coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of g-C3N4/Ag3PO4-H2O2 heterojunction system with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity and mechanism insight
coredata.dc:creator: Jia, Jiankui
Huang, Wenxin
Feng, Changsheng
Zhang, Zheng
Zuojiao, Kaichao
Liu, Jiaxun
Jiang, Caiyun
Wang, Yuping
coredata.dc:description: The stability of Ag-based photocatalysts restricted their photocatalytic performance because of their photocorrosion. To solve the problem, the g-C3N4/Ag3PO4 was fabricated through a calcination-deposition way and the novel g-C3N4/Ag3PO4-H2O2 catalytic system was constructed in the article. The cyclic experiment and X-ray diffraction peaks of Ag3PO4 and Ag3PO4-H2O2 showed that the structure stability of g-C3N4/Ag3PO4-H2O2 was protected by using H2O2 preventing the photoetching of Ag3PO4. Ultraviolet visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy declared that the construction of g-C3N4/Ag3PO4 heterojunction greatly improved the utilization of visible light. In addition, the transient photocurrent responses and photoluminescence spectra demonstrated that the photoinduced holes and electrons were separated effectively formed by g-C3N4 and Ag3PO4. Obviously, the above three processes promoted the excellent stability and photocatalytic performance of the g-C3N4/Ag3PO4-H2O2 catalytic system. The free radical trapping experiments also proved that holes played a leading role in photocatalytic degradation, and the possible mechanism of the g-C3N4/Ag3PO4-H2O2 catalytic system was proposed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: g-C3N4/Ag3PO4
H2O2
Heterojunction
Photocatalysis
Photocatalytic mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The transient photocurrent responses and PL spectra

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of the as-prepared g-C3N4; Ag3PO4 and gp-5 nanomaterials and (b) g-C3N4/Ag3PO4 nanomaterials with different content of g-C3N4.
FT-IR spectra of the as-prepared gp-5; g-C3N4 and Ag3PO4.
SEM images of the as-prepared (a) g-C3N4; (b) Ag3PO4; (c) gp-5 nanocomposite; and TEM images of (d) g-C3N4; (e) Ag3PO4; (f–g) gp-5 nanocomposite.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of g-C3N4 and gp-5; and the corresponding high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1s; (c) N 1s; (d) O 1s; (e) P 2p and (f) Ag 3d.
(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra and (b) the corresponding optical band gap of g-C3N4, gp-5 and Ag3PO4.
(a) Transient photocurrent response curves and (b) PL spectra of g-C3N4; gp-5 and Ag3PO4.

Cycling runs for the photocatalytic degradation curves over (a) Ag3PO4 and (b) Ag3PO4+H2O2; (c) the XRD patterns of Ag3PO4 after one cycle and Ag3PO4+H2O2 after 5th run cycle photocatalytic experiments; The photocatalytic degradation of (d,e) RhB based on the as-prepared samples; (f) Cycle experiments of gp-5 and gp-5+ H2O2 for photocatalytic degradation of RhB under visible-light irradiation. (The dosage of catalyst in the experiment was 20 mg, and the concentration of RhB was 15 mg/L)
Photocatalytic degradation curves of RhB over gp-5+H2O2 with various trapping scavengers (The dosage of capture agent is 1 mmol/L).
The photocatalytic mechanism diagram of g-C3N4/Ag3PO4-H2O2 under visible-light irradiation.
Schematic illustration of the preparation process for g-C3N4/Ag3PO4 nanocomposites.
Calculated value of the CB and VB potentials of g-C3N4 and Ag3PO4.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of g-C3N4/Ag3PO4-H2O2 heterojunction system with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity and mechanism insight

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Jiangsu Provincial Key Laboratory of Materials Cycling and Pollution Control, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210046, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Science
Jiangsu Provincial Key Laboratory of Materials Cycling and Pollution Control

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Engineering and Technology, Jiangsu Institute of Commerce, Nanjing 211168, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Chemical Pollution Control and Resources Reuse, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing 210094, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Chemical Pollution Control and Resources Reuse
Nanjing University of Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Material Science, Jiangsu Provincial Key Laboratory of Materials Cycling and Pollution Control, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210046, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Science
Jiangsu Provincial Key Laboratory of Materials Cycling and Pollution Control

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The stability of Ag-based photocatalysts restricted their photocatalytic performance because of their photocorrosion. To solve the problem, the g-C3N4/Ag3PO4 was fabricated through a calcination-deposition way and the novel g-C3N4/Ag3PO4-H2O2 catalytic system was constructed in the article. The cyclic experiment and X-ray diffraction peaks of Ag3PO4 and Ag3PO4-H2O2 showed that the structure stability of g-C3N4/Ag3PO4-H2O2 was protected by using H2O2 preventing the photoetching of Ag3PO4. Ultraviolet visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy declared that the construction of g-C3N4/Ag3PO4 heterojunction greatly improved the utilization of visible light. In addition, the transient photocurrent responses and photoluminescence spectra demonstrated that the photoinduced holes and electrons were separated effectively formed by g-C3N4 and Ag3PO4. Obviously, the above three processes promoted the excellent stability and photocatalytic performance of the g-C3N4/Ag3PO4-H2O2 catalytic system. The free radical trapping experiments also proved that holes played a leading role in photocatalytic degradation, and the possible mechanism of the g-C3N4/Ag3PO4-H2O2 catalytic system was proposed.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, a variety of environmental and ecological issues have arisen with the continuous development of chemical industry, especially the pollution of water resources [1]. A large number of studies showed that photocatalytic oxidation technology is expected to become a potential water treatment technology because of the characteristics of effective, safe and environmentally friendly [2,3]. Unfortunately, TiO2 [4], ZnO [5], and other semiconductor photocatalysts always have such flaws as low efficiency of charge separation and weak utilization of visible light [6], which led to negative limitations on the actual usability of photocatalysts. Therefore, many methods are considered to explore novel semiconductor heterostructure running under visible light [7].

Recently, the graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has the advantages of facile preparation method and chemical stability, which has arisen the interest of researchers. The g-C3N4 is widely used to degrade pollutants [8] and produce H2 in water [9]. However, the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4 is confined on account of its low conductivity and rapid recombination of electron hole pairs. Therefore, in order to further optimize the practical performance of g-C3N4, numerous pathways have been used to optimize the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4, such as metal doping [10–12], non-metal loading [13–15], morphology control [10], and semiconductor coupling [16–18]. In the above method, the build of heterojunction is a very practical method to improve the photocatalytic performance of catalysts. For example, Yan et al. [19] synthesized microspheric g-C3N4-Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst with excellent photocatalytic properties by a template-free solvothermal method. You et al. [20] reported that g-C3N4/InVO4 heterojunction was prepared by a simple template-free method, and exhibited enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activities. Therefore, further research on the novel g-C3N4-based photocatalyst is still indispensable and deserved.

It was reported that Ag-based semiconductors photocatalysts including Ag2CO3 [21], Ag2O [22], Ag2S [23], and Ag3PO4 [24–26] were well-known visible-light-driven photocatalyst, and when they were combined with other semiconductors, the heterostructure photocatalysts exhibited excellent photocatalytic efficiency. Particularly, g-C3N4/Ag-based catalysts, such as g-C3N4/Ag3PO4/NCDs [27], g-C3N4/Ag2CO3 [28] and Ag2O/g-C3N4 [29] were proved to have optimized photocatalytic performance. However, the stability of silver-based photocatalysts restricts their photocatalytic performance because the Ag+ contained in Ag-based photocatalysts can be easily converted to Ag when exposed to visible light [30,31]. In view of this, it is indispensable and important to create novel g-C3N4/Ag-based composite with good stability as well as high catalytic activity working under visible light.

In this paper, in order to develop new g-C3N4/Ag-based photocatalyst and overcome the photoetching phenomenon of Ag-based semiconductor material, we report a new heterojunction system of g-C3N4/Ag3PO4-H2O2 and the g-C3N4/Ag3PO4 is prepared through a calcination-deposition way. The g-C3N4/Ag3PO4-H2O2 heterojunction system exhibits higher photocatalytic efficiency than g-C3N4, g-C3N4-H2O2, Ag3PO4-H2O2 and g-C3N4/Ag3PO4. Meanwhile, the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4/Ag3PO4-H2O2 is basically unchanged after five cycles of experiments, indicating a high stability of the g-C3N4/Ag3PO4-H2O2 photocatalyst. In addition, the physicochemical properties of the composites are characterized. The possible mechanism of the photocatalytic reaction is also proposed according to the experimental analysis.

Melamine, silver nitrate, absolute ethyl alcohol, p-benzoquinone, isopropanol and hydrogen peroxide (30 wt%), were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4-12H2O) was purchased from Shantou west long chemical plant. Rhodamine B (RhB) was purchased form Tianjin Chemical Reagent Research Institute and tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) was obtained from BBI Life Sciences Corporation. All of reagents are analytical grade in this study and used without further purification. All experimental water is deionized (DI) water. All the abbreviations mentioned are put at the end of the article.

The g-C3N4 was synthesized by the improved calcination method in the literature [32]. In brief, a porcelain crucible containing 6 g melamine with a cover was placed in a muffle furnace and calcined at 520 °C for 4 h, and then the g-C3N4 was collected and pulverized with an agate mortar for further use.

The g-C3N4/Ag3PO4 was synthesized by a simple deposition-precipitation method. Specifically, different amount of g-C3N4 was dissolved in 30 mL of DI water by ultrasonication for 60min. Then, 1.2184 g of AgNO3 was dissolved into the above solution and continuing ultrasonication for another 30 min to enable Ag+ ions evenly dispersed in the solution. Thereafter, 17.2 mL of Na2HPO4 solution (50 mg/mL) was added to the above reaction and magnetic stirring for 60 min at 60 °C. The g-C3N4/Ag3PO4 composites were collected with the suction filter, and then washed several times with DI water and dried at 70 °C for 12 h. The final product was pulverized with an agate mortar and denoted as g-C3N4/Ag3PO4. In addition, the samples containing g-C3N4 with different mass ratios (2%, 5%, 10%, 20%, and 50%) were denoted as gp-2, gp-5, gp-10, gp-20, gp-50, respectively. The synthetic way of the g-C3N4/Ag3PO4 composite is represented in Scheme 1 .
For comparison, the pure Ag3PO4 crystal was prepared by the method above in the absence of g-C3N4.

The crystal structures and composition of the as-prepared materials were determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku D/max 2500VL/PC). The physiognomy and microstructures of the catalysts were conducted by scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-5610LV) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM ZEOL JEM-2100). The functional groups of the samples were detected by a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (HYPERION 2000). The XPS spectrum (Thermo Fisher Escalab 250xi) was used to analyze the surface chemical composition of the materials. The absorption range and band gap of the catalysts were measured by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Cary 5000) with BaSO4 as a reflectance. And the HORIBA FM-4P-TCSPC spectrometer was used to detect the fluorescence intensity of the samples. The photocurrent response values of the materials were measured by electrochemical workstation (CHI 660E), and 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution was used as the electrolyte.

The photocatalytic properties of the synthesized samples were detected by removing the aqueous solution of RhB and TC under irradiation of 800 W Xe lamp with a 420 nm cutoff filter. The XPA-7 photochemical reactor (Nanjing Xujiang electromechanical plant, Nanjing, China) was employed for the experimental study of photodegradation. The lamp is placed in the quartz trap of the center of the instrument with cooling water, and the quartz tube containing the solution is about 10 cm away from the lamp. Typically, 20 mg of the sample was mixed in 50 mL of RhB (15 mg/L) or TC (50 mg/L) solution. Prior to illumination, the mixed reaction system was placed in the dark and stirred for 30 min to stabilize an adsorption-desorption equilibrium (Fig. S1). Meanwhile, 0.1 mL hydrogen peroxide (30 wt%) was dissolved in the above mixed solution. During the photocatalytic degradation, the mixed solution (5 mL) was taken out as a sample at 10 min interval and centrifuged. The remaining concentration of RhB and TC was detected by an UV–vis spectrophotometer at the absorption wavelength of 554 nm and 360 nm, respectively. The degradation percentage (D) after each experiment was calculated based on Eq. (1) as follows:
(1)
D =
[
(
C
0
−
C
t
)
/
C
0
]
× 100 %
Where
C
0
is the original absorbance of the pollutant at t = 0 and
C
t
is the instant absorbance at photocatalytic degradation time t (min).

Fig. 1 shows the XRD data of pure g-C3N4, Ag3PO4 and g-C3N4/Ag3PO4 with different amounts of g-C3N4. For pure g-C3N4 (Fig. 1a), the diffraction peaks appear at 13.11° and 27.54°are consistent with the g-C3N4 (100) and (002) crystal planes, respectively, and the two peaks are consistent with the internal connection of the triazine units and the accumulation of the aromatic system, respectively [33]. All the diffraction peaks of Ag3PO4 are assigned to the cubic phase Ag3PO4 (JCPDS No. 06-0505). The characteristic patterns of g-C3N4/Ag3PO4 show similar with that of the Ag3PO4. The characteristic peaks of g-C3N4 are not shown in gp-5 composite, which may be due to the poor crystallinity and the low content of g-C3N4 in gp-5 [34]. The experimental results are also consistent with the BET results (Fig. S2). BET data indicate that due to the low levels of g-C3N4 in the complex, the specific surface area of gp-5 is a little higher than that of Ag3PO4, so it could not provide more active sites. In addition, Fig. 1b shows the XRD patterns of g-C3N4/Ag3PO4 with different proportions of g-C3N4. It can be found that the characteristic peak of g-C3N4 gradually appears with the increase of g-C3N4 percentage, which further indicates that Ag3PO4 forms a complex with g-C3N4 (Fig. S3). Simultaneously, no peaks of other substances are observed in these samples.

Fig. 2 shows the FT-IR functional group curves of the prepared g-C3N4, Ag3PO4 and gp-5. It can be seen from the peaks of Ag3PO4 that the peak at 1016 cm−1 is consistent with the P-O-P stretching vibrations [35,36], and the band at 562 cm−1 corresponds to the bending vibrations of the OPO [37]. For the curve of g-C3N4, the diagnostic peaks detected in 1408–1643 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of the C-N and CN [38], and the characteristic peak at 808 cm−1 corresponds to the triazine vibrations of g-C3N4 [10]. It can be found that the main peaks of Ag3PO4 and the characteristic peaks corresponding to g-C3N4 exist in the spectrum of the gp-5 composite, which implies that the synthesis of the composite is successful.

As shown in Fig. 3 , the surface morphology and microstructure of g-C3N4, Ag3PO4 and gp-5 are described by the SEM and TEM images. Fig. 3a shows that g-C3N4 has a slice network structure with large surface area, which is consistent with the BET measurements and the literature [39]. In Fig. 3b, irregular Ag3PO4 nanoparticles have a certain degree of aggregation, and the size of Ag3PO4 is about 200–400 nm [27]. As displayed in Fig. 3c, irregular Ag3PO4 spheres were loaded on the surface of g-C3N4 sheets. In addition, it can be found that the physiognomy and size of samples (Fig. 3d–g) are well consistent with the above SEM analysis. The above analysis confirm the successful assembly of g-C3N4/Ag3PO4 composite.

Fig. 4 shows XPS spectra of g-C3N4 and gp-5. In Fig. 4a, the elements of C, N, O, P and Ag are found in the gp-5 composite and the elements of C and N are recorded in the g-C3N4, which correspond to the composition of the samples. The C1s spectrum of all the samples (Fig. 4b) shows two peaks at 284.4 and 286.3 eV, corresponding to CC bonds and COH bonds of the surface carbon, respectively [40]. The XPS diagnostic peaks at 287.9 eV of gp-5 and 288.0 eV of g-C3N4 are ascribed to the CN bonds of g-C3N4. The slight negative shift of C 1 s peak (by 0.1 eV) may be affected by the charge carrier separation formed by g-C3N4 and Ag3PO4 [41], and the relative intensity of the CN3 peaks in gp-5 sample is lower than that of pure g-C3N4 due to the lower content of g-C3N4. As observed in Fig. 4c, the three peaks at 398.5 eV, 399.7 eV and 400.9 eV were detected in the N 1s spectra of all the sample, which correspond to C-NC, SP3-hybridized nitrogen (NC3) and NHx groups in g-C3N4, respectively [27]. And the relative intensity of the three peaks in gp-5 sample is lower than pure g-C3N4 because of the lower content of g-C3N4. In Fig. 4d, there are three peaks at approximate 530.1 eV, 531.0 eV and 532.0 eV corresponding to O2− in gp-5 [42], OH group and H2O molecules on the surface of gp-5 [40], respectively. The bond energy of P2p observed at 132.52 eV is shown in Fig. 4e, which is consistent with the existence of P5+ of Ag3PO4 in gp-5 [43,44]. Meanwhile, the peaks (Fig. 4f) at 367.6 eV and 373.8 eV of Ag 3d spectrum attribute to the binding energies of Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2, respectively, which confirm that Ag+ successfully loaded in gp-5 [45]. The XPS analysis results further confirm that the g-C3N4/Ag3PO4 composites are successfully synthesized.

The UV–vis DRS spectra (Fig. 5 ) of the as-prepared samples were provided by a UV–vis spectrophotometer. The band gap of g-C3N4 is about 2.65 eV (Fig. 5b) because of its absorption edge of 462 nm in Fig. 5a, which attributes to the previous results [46]. As displayed in Fig. 5a, the pure Ag3PO4 shows a high absorption range at about 550 nm, corresponding to its band gap of 2.15 eV (Fig. 5b). When Ag3PO4 nanoparticles are combined with g-C3N4, gp-5 shows an improved visible light absorption wavelength, which is related to the small band-gap of Ag3PO4. This result suggests that the visible-light utilization efficiency of the composite was greatly improved due to the introduction of Ag3PO4 (red line in Fig. 5a).

Based on the data of Fig. 5, the energy levels of g-C3N4 and Ag3PO4 are calculated by the following formulas (Eqs. (2) and (3)) [47–49]:
(2)
E
V B
= X −
E
e
+ 0.5
E
g
(3)
E
C B
=
E
V B
−
E
g
Where
E
e
is 4.5 eV,
X
is the absolute electronegativity of catalyst;
E
V B
and
E
C B
are the VB and CB edge potential, respectively;
E
g
is the band gap energy of catalyst. Based on formulas (2) and (3), the calculated values of ECB and EVB of g-C3N4 and Ag3PO4 are shown in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: The transient photocurrent responses and PL spectra
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 a shows the transient photocurrent responses of the as-prepared samples in four switching cycles under transitory visible light with 40s cycle interval. Clearly, the photocurrent value of gp-5 is higher than pure Ag3PO4 and g-C3N4, indicating that gp-5 possesses more efficient charge carrier transfer. In order to better understand the valid separation of photoelectric charge carriers of the above materials. The PL spectra of the prepared g-C3N4, Ag3PO4, and gp-5 materials are measured at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. The strong emission peak (Fig. 6b) at about 475 nm is detected for g-C3N4, which attributes to its band gap energy. However, the fluorescence emission intensity of gp-5 is significantly lower than that of g-C3N4. The above analyses indicate that the photoinduced holes and electrons are separated effectively. Thus, the photocatalytic performance of the catalysts is improved, which corresponds to the above experimental and catalyst characterization results.

Although Ag3PO4 nanoparticles exhibit excellent photocatalytic property under visible-light irradiation, the stability issue caused by photocorrosion still exists (Ag3PO4 + 3e−→ 3Ag + PO4 3−, Eθ (Standard electrode potential) = 0.45 eV vs NHE (Normal Hydrogen Electrode)). Fig. 7 shows the photocatalytic efficiency of different catalysts for degrading RhB under visible-light irradiation. It can be seen from Fig. 7a ,b that the pure Ag3PO4 shows superior photocatalytic activity compared with Ag3PO4-H2O2 in the first photodegradation process, the RhB aqueous solution of 15 mg/L could be completely degraded within 30 min. However, it is found in cyclic experimental that the decomposition efficiency of RhB is basically unchanged after five cycles by Ag3PO4-H2O2 system and it is greatly reduced by Ag3PO4 catalyst after five cycles, indicating that the addition of H2O2 can inhibit the photoetching of Ag3PO4 and enhance its stability.

In order to analyze the function of H2O2 in photocatalytic reaction, firstly, the impact of the amount of hydrogen peroxide on photocatalytic activity of Ag3PO4 is investigated, and the experiment results are shown in Fig. S4. The removal efficiency of RhB by Ag3PO4 with different dosage (0.05, 0.1, 0.15 and 0.2 mL) of H2O2 are about 57.2%, 57.6%, 56.1%, 58.2%, respectively. This results illustrate that the dosage of H2O2 has no influence on photocatalytic removal efficiency. Secondly, the structure of Ag3PO4 in different system is characterized and shows in Fig. 7c. The characteristic peaks at 38.15°, 44.29°, 64.54°and 77.46°, matching to metallic Ag0 [50,51], are detected in the XRD diffraction peaks of pure Ag3PO4 after one cycles. On the contrary, no peaks of Ag appear in the XRD diffraction peaks of Ag3PO4-H2O2 after five cycles (see Fig. 7c and inset). This results show that the more and more Ag granules are produced on the surface of pure Ag3PO4 under continuous illumination, which not only hinder the absorption and utilization of incident light by Ag3PO4, but also prevent the effective charge carrier transfer and separation. Finally, the photocatalytic activity of Ag3PO4 is greatly reduced (Fig. 7a). In the Ag3PO4-H2O2 system, the H2O2 molecules are easier to combine with e− than Ag3PO4 (H2O2 + e−→OH +OH− Eθ = 0.87 eV) due to the more negative reduction potential of OH/H2O2 than that of the Ag/Ag3PO4 [52]. As a result, the optical corrosion phenomenon of Ag3PO4 is inhibited. In addition, the photocatalytic properties of all samples are tested under the irradiation of visible light. The experimental data are shown in Fig. 7d, the degradation efficiency of RhB solution is basically unchanged in the absence of catalysts, RhB is unable oxidized by H2O2 and the gp-5+H2O2 sample exhibits the highest photocatalytic degradation capacity for RhB. The removal rate of RhB is only 20% by g-C3N4 photocatalyst, however, the decomposition rate of RhB is increased to 39% in the presence of H2O2, the experimental results are consistent with those reported in the literature [53], indicating hydrogen peroxide has synergistic effect with g-C3N4 catalyst. Compared with the photocatalytic degradation of gp-5 and gp-5+H2O2, it can be found that H2O2 hardly affected the first excellent photocatalytic activity of gp-5. The photodegradation efficiency of RhB by g-C3N4/Ag3PO4-H2O2 system with different proportions of g-C3N4 is shown in Fig. 7e, g-C3N4/Ag3PO4-H2O2 containing 5% g-C3N4 has the highest decomposition efficiency for RhB, indicating that the combination of the two catalysts has an appropriate proportion. The degradation efficiency of g-C3N4/Ag3PO4-H2O2 will decrease with excess g-C3N4 is introduced to the composites. It may be ascribed to that overmuch g-C3N4 nanosheets cover the active sites of Ag3PO4, which impedes the efficient charge carrier transfer on the surface of the g-C3N4/Ag3PO4 composites.

The experimental results of five cycles of gp-5 and gp-5+H2O2 system are shown in Fig. 7f. It can be seen that the photocatalytic activity of gp-5+H2O2 remains basically unchanged after five photocatalytic degradation cycles, indicating that the gp-5+H2O2 system has a better stability than gp-5 in terms of photocatalytic degradation. The decomposition efficiency of TC by gp-5+H2O2 system is shown in Fig. S5. The removal rate of TC (50 mg/L) reached 83% within 20 min under the irradiation of visible light. The above analysis shows that the g-C3N4/Ag3PO4-H2O2 system also has excellent photocatalytic degradation ability for other pollutants. In order to prove the photo-efficiency of the studied materials, the photocatalytic degradation of RhB by gp-5 is also carried out at different higher volume of initial solution with the same dosage. The experimental results (Fig. S6) show that the experiment can be successfully achieved at different higher volume and the results are relatively consistent, further demonstrating the excellent photocatalytic activity of gp-5.

As is known to all, holes (h+), superoxide ion radicals (O2 −) and hydroxyl radicals (OH) are common reactive species in photocatalytic degradation process [54]. For better understanding the mechanism of the reaction system, the free radical trapping experiments were carried out with different collectors to study the main active components in the photocatalytic reaction. As exhibited in Fig. 8 , disodium ethylenediamine tetraacetate (Na2-EDTA), benzoquinone (BQ) and isopropanol (IPA) are employed to remove h+, O2 − and OH, respectively [53,55]. Fig. 8 shows the removal efficiency of RhB by gp-5+H2O2 with various trapping agents, the photodegradation efficiency of RhB solution is remarkably suppressed when adding the Na2-EDTA scavengers, which indicates that h+ plays a major role in the reaction process. The addition of BQ has little influence on the removal rate of RhB, suggesting that the O2 − is the subsidiary active substance in reaction system. In addition, the introduction of IPA has a super weak effect on RhB degradation efficiency, indicating that the H2O2 is converted into OH after capturing the small amount of electrons of g-C3N4 and Ag3PO4 (H2O2 + e−→OH + OH−) [53]. Therefore, it can be seen that the h+ plays a major role, the O2 − plays an auxiliary role and the OH is the lesser active substance in this reactions system.

Based on the experimental results above, the possible reaction mechanism of the g-C3N4/Ag3PO4-H2O2 system is predicted and revealed in Fig. 9 . When g-C3N4 and Ag3PO4 are irradiated by visible light, the photogenerated electrons of them are excited from the valence band to the corresponding conduction band, respectively. Firstly, the photoinduced electrons of Ag3PO4 can be partially transferred to the interface of g-C3N4 and quickly recombine with the h+ in the VB of g-C3N4 on account of the suitable energy band match in the g-C3N4/Ag3PO4 heterojunction [56], resulting in the effective separation of e− in the CB of g-C3N4 and h+ in the VB of Ag3PO4. Meanwhile, owing to the more negative CB potential of g-C3N4 than that of the O2/O2 −, dissolved O2 is reduced by the electrons on g-C3N4 to form O2 − in the reaction system. Subsequently, O2 − can oxidize the pollutants directly, and the photogenerated holes of Ag3PO4 can directly oxidize pollutants into H2O and CO2 due to its excellent oxidation performance [57]. In addition, under the protection of H2O2, the structure of Ag3PO4 is protected by trapping the residual photoinduced electrons of Ag3PO4 to prevent the formation of Ag particles, which is attributed to the more negative reduction potential of OH/H2O2 (Eθ(OH/H2O2) = 0.87 eV) than that of the Ag/Ag3PO4. As a consequence, g-C3N4/Ag3PO4-H2O2 system exhibits improved photocatalytic performance and structural stability.

Based on the experiment analysis above, the possible reaction formulas are described in Eqs. (4)–(7).
(4)
g p − 5 + h ν →
e
−
+
h
+
(5)
(6)
(7)

To sum up, the g-C3N4/Ag3PO4 photocatalyst with enhanced visible light utilization was fabricated by a calcination-deposition method. The physicochemical properties of the composites were characterized. SEM and TEM images shown that g-C3N4 presented a flaky network structure, and irregular Ag3PO4 spheres were loaded on the surface of g-C3N4 sheets. When the g-C3N4/Ag3PO4-H2O2 heterojunction system was constructed by adding H2O2, the gp-5+H2O2 exhibited improved photocatalytic performance and structural stability for RhB and TC degradation, which attributes to the enhanced utilization rate of visible light, the effective charge carrier transfer of the composite and the structure stability of catalyst using H2O2 preventing the photoetching of Ag3PO4 in the heterojunction system. Therefore, the development of novel heterojunction system is expected to be a feasible pathway to effectively degrade organic pollutants by solar energy.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 51578295), the National Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20161479), and Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Chemical Pollution Control and Resources Reuse (Nanjing University of Science and Technology), Qing Lan Project of Jiangsu Province. Additionally, we also appreciate Foundation of Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials and a project funded by the priority academic program development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2019.03.238.
